text_clean: Just incredible. The fortress of the Chachapoyas culture will leave you amazed. The aperture of the cableway (for only 20 soles and 20 minutes, not for vertigo sufferers) makes easier the visit to this archeological site for older people and children. But for those who love trekking you can always take the 3 to 4 hour way to the fortress. It's a must go when visiting Amazonas. The entrance is up to 20 soles for adults. To get to the top you must walk 30 minutes o less or you can rent a horse. Guide is optional.

text_clean: It’s not a criticism of the place but the people that run it. As usual in Peru the info you can see on Google hasn’t been updated in a long time. It would take them less than 2 minutes and that would help many gringos that prepare their journey with info they find on internet but no, they can’t be bothered even tho they live from tourism and are always looking to get more money from the tourists. The minimum effort on their part would be to actually update the info, a thing that I’ve done now three times instead of the actual f. people who work in those touristic places cause I thought that would avoid other people getting the same disappointment I had. Also I did ask the guy who was selling the tickets and announced me ‘oh no this timetable is from before the pandemic’ : Me > ‘so I’m curious, why haven’t you updated the timetable info then?’ Him > just shrugging shoulders. Yeah.

The far opposite, when it finished it seem like the hour has just gone in a moment. I do not remember the name of the guide, but she was so knowledgeable and professional

text_clean: La visita guiada ha sido correcta pero le faltaba más explicaciones sobre las distintas culturas existentes. La huaca es magnifica digna de ver al visitar Lima. Al final de la visita tienen un zoo con cuys y alpacas con poco espacio y no tiene ningún sentido en un sitio arqueológico. Es inaceptable el estado de los animales, siendo ademas las alpacas de zonas frias y no calidas como Lima.

text_clean: This is my third time here and let me tell you there’s been tons of changes. After you get to Aguas Calientes, everyone whether you hired a guide or not must now go to the Ministerio de Culturas and stand in line just to get a number to return later that night or the next day. My understanding from talking to other tour guides is that tour guides used to be able to buy your tickets and they would buy at the Peruvian National price and resell at the international tourist price. To cut down on this corruption now everyone must go and present their passports or their proof of residency (DNI). You are asked how many people in your party and you are given individual numbers for each person. You cannot show up early as they go by the number you were given. Their computer systems are not very stable so expect delays. We were in line a little over 2 hours in the morning and over 3 hours in the evening so almost 6 hours. When your number is called, you go upstairs and have to wait in and move yourself along the winding chairs that lead you back down the stairs to the cashier windows to be asked what circuit you will be getting. There are limited number of tickets per circuit and even though there are “maps” there is nothing showing you what you will see in each circuit and what your pictures will look like. There are screens that can be looked to determine which circuits are still available as you approach the window to place your order. Fee per permit/ticket is $50/person for international traveler regardless of the circuit chosen. Circuit 2A/B is quite popular so for me, I had to go with circuit 3A and that’s the picture I am sharing on this review. Previously on my other 2 visits, I was able to do the whole trip as a day trip and see Machu Picchu and return to Cusco the same day. Now you must stay overnight and some people up to two nights so plan carefully. That said, this area is a lower altitude than Cusco so it is easier to breathe and the town is much more quaint and more enjoyable. That said do not buy your return tickets to Cusco until you have your tour time because you are only allowed to make changes up to 24 hours before and if you try to change at 23 hours and 59 minutes, you will have to buy new tickets. Another piece of advice, when booking bus/train tickets go with Peru Rail instead of Inca Rail as the train cars are much nicer and have wider aisles and better “shows”. With these changes, it almost makes it not worth it to even visit. To me, Machu Picchu is completely overrated and overpriced and inconvenient to get to. Although I’m glad I’ve come 3 times (first time 2021 during covid and raining so pictures were not adequate, second time 2023 with niece and third time 2025 with my wife because she wanted to visit), I’ve thought this every time I’ve come.

When your number is called, you go upstairs and have to wait in and move yourself along the winding chairs that lead you back down the stairs to the cashier windows to be asked what circuit you will be getting. There are limited number of tickets per circuit and even though there are “maps” there is nothing showing you what you will see in each circuit and what your pictures will look like. There are screens that can be looked to determine which circuits are still available as you approach the window to place your order. Fee per permit/ticket is $50/person for international traveler regardless of the circuit chosen. Circuit 2A/B is quite popular so for me, I had to go with circuit 3A and that’s the picture I am sharing on this review. Previously on my other 2 visits, I was able to do the whole trip as a day trip and see Machu Picchu and return to Cusco the same day. Now you must stay overnight and some people up to two nights so plan carefully. That said, this area is a lower altitude than Cusco so it is easier to breathe and the town is much more quaint and more enjoyable. That said do not buy your return tickets to Cusco until you have your tour time because you are only allowed to make changes up to 24 hours before and if you try to change at 23 hours and 59 minutes, you will have to buy new tickets. Another piece of advice, when booking bus/train tickets go with Peru Rail instead of Inca Rail as the train cars are much nicer and have wider aisles and better “shows”. With these changes, it almost makes it not worth it to even visit. To me, Machu Picchu is completely overrated and overpriced and inconvenient to get to. Although I’m glad I’ve come 3 times (first time 2021 during covid and raining so pictures were not adequate, second time 2023 with niece and third time 2025 with my wife because she wanted to visit), I’ve thought this every time I’ve come.

text_clean: I recently had the incredible opportunity to visit the Huaca Pucllana Site Museum in Lima, and I must say it was an absolutely fascinating and enriching experience that left me in awe. This hidden gem in the heart of Lima offers a window into the rich history and culture of ancient Peru, and I can't recommend it enough to anyone visiting this vibrant city. From the moment I stepped onto the grounds of Huaca Pucllana, I was transported back in time to the era of the Lima culture that thrived here over a thousand years ago. The meticulously preserved archaeological site is a testament to the remarkable architectural and engineering skills of its ancient inhabitants. The massive adobe pyramid, the centerpiece of the site, stands proudly against the backdrop of the modern city, creating a striking contrast that serves as a reminder of Lima's deep historical roots. The guided tour I took was informative and engaging, with knowledgeable guides who shared captivating stories about the site's history, rituals, and the daily life of the people who once called it home. I was particularly impressed by the museum's dedication to preserving the site's authenticity while providing a comprehensive educational experience for visitors. One of the highlights of my visit was the opportunity to explore the site up close, including walking on top of the pyramid, which offers a breathtaking panoramic view of Lima. The sunset tour I chose provided a magical atmosphere, and I can't emphasize enough how spectacular it was to witness the city's lights come to life as the day transitioned into night. The on-site restaurant, with its exquisite Peruvian cuisine, was the perfect way to conclude my visit. I savored traditional dishes that were not only delicious but also a homage to the region's culinary heritage. It's worth noting that the site can be quite dusty, which may not be ideal for keeping one's shoes perfectly clean. However, this minor inconvenience pales in comparison to the rich historical and cultural treasures you'll uncover during your visit. It's advisable to wear comfortable and perhaps older footwear to fully enjoy the experience without worrying about dust. In conclusion, the Huaca Pucllana Site Museum is a must-visit destination in Lima. It's a place where history, culture, and gastronomy converge to create an unforgettable experience. Whether you're a history enthusiast, a food lover, or simply looking to explore the hidden treasures of Lima, Huaca Pucllana will leave you with cherished memories and a deeper appreciation for Peru's rich heritage. Don't miss the opportunity to step back in time and witness the magic of this extraordinary site.

The meticulously preserved archaeological site is a testament to the remarkable architectural and engineering skills of its ancient inhabitants. The massive adobe pyramid, the centerpiece of the site, stands proudly against the backdrop of the modern city, creating a striking contrast that serves as a reminder of Lima's deep historical roots. The guided tour I took was informative and engaging, with knowledgeable guides who shared captivating stories about the site's history, rituals, and the daily life of the people who once called it home. I was particularly impressed by the museum's dedication to preserving the site's authenticity while providing a comprehensive educational experience for visitors. One of the highlights of my visit was the opportunity to explore the site up close, including walking on top of the pyramid, which offers a breathtaking panoramic view of Lima. The sunset tour I chose provided a magical atmosphere, and I can't emphasize enough how spectacular it was to witness the city's lights come to life as the day transitioned into night. The on-site restaurant, with its exquisite Peruvian cuisine, was the perfect way to conclude my visit. I savored traditional dishes that were not only delicious but also a homage to the region's culinary heritage. It's worth noting that the site can be quite dusty, which may not be ideal for keeping one's shoes perfectly clean. However, this minor inconvenience pales in comparison to the rich historical and cultural treasures you'll uncover during your visit. It's advisable to wear comfortable and perhaps older footwear to fully enjoy the experience without worrying about dust. In conclusion, the Huaca Pucllana Site Museum is a must-visit destination in Lima. It's a place where history, culture, and gastronomy converge to create an unforgettable experience. Whether you're a history enthusiast, a food lover, or simply looking to explore the hidden treasures of Lima, Huaca Pucllana will leave you with cherished memories and a deeper appreciation for Peru's rich heritage. Don't miss the opportunity to step back in time and witness the magic of this extraordinary site.
label: positivo

text_clean: No existe comunicación entre el teleferico y Kuelap, a pesar de ser la única opción para llegar (sino debes caminar más de 4 hrs). No compren las entradas por adelantado, si te pasas de la hora no te dejan entrar y si hay algún problema no te cambian la entrada. En el teleferico hay un pequeño stand y ahi puedes comprarlas (solo por internet!) Es un bello lugar pero la administración deja mucho que desear, solo puedes hacer 1 recorrido, así que hazlo lento porque no te permiten retroceder en el camino. Me voy con la sensación de que solo les interesa el dinero y no se preocupan del turista, ni de la experiencia vivida.

text_clean: Para ir primero, toman carro en la terminal hacia kuelap que está 7 soles. Luego toman el teleferico que está 20,40 soles por persona que incluye ida y vuelta en la couster y teleférico 🚡 y que solo lo pueden usar hasta la hora que te indican, sino deberás volver a pagar los 20,40 soles para regresar. Al llegar deben pagar 30 soles por persona adulta para ingresar a kuelap, y finalmente subir a kuelap que es 30 minutos de caminata hacia arriba. De bajada es más rápido. A mi opinión personal, pienso que está sobrevalorado el costo de ingresar, ya que hay miradores con árboles de eucalipto y frutos en el camino, donde la entrada es 3 soles y que están cerca, asimismo podrían ir a las cataratas de gocta más temprano o los miradores de huancas y uno más que no me acuerdo. La verdad, que más me emocionó subir al teleferico 🚡 que llegar a kuelap, que fue la única decepción de mi viaje, además que ya no permiten ingresar a las casitas para tomar foto. Antes se podía según lo que me contó una persona que ya visitaba el lugar por segunda vez. Olvidaba, para ir a kuelap NO TOMEN GUIADO NI TOUR. no es necesario, es fácil de llegar.

A mi opinión personal, pienso que está sobrevalorado el costo de ingresar, ya que hay miradores con árboles de eucalipto y frutos en el camino, donde la entrada es 3 soles y que están cerca, asimismo podrían ir a las cataratas de gocta más temprano o los miradores de huancas y uno más que no me acuerdo. La verdad, que más me emocionó subir al teleferico 🚡 que llegar a kuelap, que fue la única decepción de mi viaje, además que ya no permiten ingresar a las casitas para tomar foto. Antes se podía según lo que me contó una persona que ya visitaba el lugar por segunda vez. Olvidaba, para ir a kuelap NO TOMEN GUIADO NI TOUR. no es necesario, es fácil de llegar.
label: negativo

text_clean: Not to be missed of you're traveling in Arequipa and even better to make to the trip from Arequipa to Puno (or the other way around) much more interesting. Amazing landscapes going from tropical desert to alti plano, the agricultural terrasses in the value and the deepest canyon in the world. The absolute highlight was the condor viewing point where these beautiful animal fly few meters above your head. We took a two day tour Arequipa - Colca - Puno. Costed 87usd pp.

text_clean: Parque de la.Reserva, es un parque donde su mayor atracción esta en el show de agua, tiene dos estaciones principales y en especial hay un show donde muestran el agua con diferentes colores, formas y además reflejan máscaras o elementos representativos de la región. Es un show muy lindo y me parece muy importante resaltar que en Perú siempre en sus shows, artesanías etc siempre están dando a conocer los elementos representativos de su país. El show dura 15 minutos Valor de la entrada 4 soles, el cual es bastante adsequible Tiene diferentes juegos para los niños Espacios para comer Tiene zona de parqueo Yo fui el viernes y por ser un día festivo, no recomiendo ir ese tipo de días porque es extremadamente lleno, por ende las filas son muy largas, es un poco difícil apreciar el show y es difícil transitar entonces tuvimos que marcharnos después del show y no pudimos seguir viendo con calma las demás cosas que tiene el parque. No hay buena señalización para salir del parque, deberían colocar avisos con luces de neón para ubicarse más fácil, los letreros de salida están muy al piso entonces no se ven.

Highly recommended for history buffs and nature lovers alike!

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: The Royal Tombs of Sipán Museum near Chiclayo is a must-visit if you love history and archaeology. The pyramid-style architecture itself reflects the ancient Moche culture, and inside you walk through the story of the Lord of Sipán step by step, from excavation to the stunning treasures found in his tomb. The gold, silver, ceramics, and textiles give a deep insight into the artistry and beliefs of the Mochica civilization. The museum is very well maintained — clean, organized, and carefully curated with proper lighting and security that protect these priceless artifacts. The exhibits are presented with respect and detail, making it easy to appreciate the importance of this culture in Peru’s history. Some explanations are only in Spanish and weekends can be a bit crowded, but overall it is one of the best museums in Peru and highly recommended 😊😊😊

To sum up: if you came here specifically to visit the lines, I guess you will like it, go for it. If you’re a casual tourist explorer like us, probably also ok to skip and do something else. Hope it helps to those who have a strong fear of missing out like me! :)

text_clean: English y español (second paragraph). Be careful with this tourist trap, you will need a reservation to go in, however, this is not well explained on the normal internet search. Here is the scheme, a lot of tourists, mostly English speaking come without reservation and there are a couple of ”tourists guides", taxi drivers in reality impersonating being part of the museum staff, at the entrance letting you know that you will not be able to enter without the reservation. In exchange, they can take to another archeological site for a significant amount of money (varies from 400 to 250 PENS). If you try to make it to the reception window the taxi driver cut you off and let them know that you don't have reservations with the typical wink wink, so they don't do much to help you. At the end, if there are space available or no shows, you might get access to the museum. So, my recommendation is, if you don't have a reservation, do not even bother to show up. Now, the museum inside is ok, you will learn about the pre-inka civilization in the area and their worship to the god of the sea. The your is mandatory, it last just one hour, and is quite entertaining, if you are into this kind of attractions. Cuidado con esta trampa para turistas. TIENE que tener reservación para entrar al museo. Si no tiene reservación vas a encontrar a un par de buitres con carnet de taxistas haciéndose pasar por guías turísticos y empleados del museo (que no son) para llevarte a otros sitios arqueológicos a 30 minutos en carro por una suma considerable suma (250 a 400 soles). Los taxistas se comunican con los empleados del museo para dejarlos saber que no tienes reserva con el típico guiño de ojo para que no traten de ayudarte mucho para clavarte con el tour del taxista. Al final, so hay cupo y presentas presión, en el museo te venden los tickets, pero eso es ya cosa de suerte. Asi, que si no tienes reservación, ni trates de llegar al museo, a menos que te quieras correr la suerte. Ahora, ya dentro del museo, el tour es mandatario, y esta muy entretenido. Sólo dura una hora.

text_clean: Kuélap is absolutely amazing! The place is full of energy and history, surrounded by stunning vegetation and mountain views. The whole tour inside takes about 2 hours, with lots of ruins and impressive walls to explore. Entrance is S/ 20 (online only). To get there you have three options: • Hiking (very hard), • By car (Nogalcucho or Choctamal), • Or the cable car, which we chose and highly recommend! It costs around S/ 27 per person, takes about 25 minutes, and offers breathtaking views. Important note: Kuélap is not only a fortress, but a whole fortified city built by the Chachapoyas culture around 900–1100 AD. The cable car itself was built with French technology and is super modern and safe. Definitely one of the most impressive experiences in northern Peru!

text_clean: Truly wonderful. A must see to check off from the travel list. Plan to stay a night in aguas calientes. Take a private tour guide with you (highly recommended). They can be hired at the time of boarding the bus in aguas calientes. There is room to negotiate the price and should cost you around $50-60 USD, money well spent IMO. Without the tour guide you will just be looking at rock structures with no context to why it was built an how it was used. More tips - 1> There is no direct train to aguas calientes from Cusco. Spend a night in Ollantaytambo and take the train from there. Most hotels there will keep your heavy luggage for a day or 2 if needed for free. 2> Peru Rail has more frequent trips so choose that instead of Inca rail although either one is fine. 3> Do not fall for the Vistadome and Vistadome Observatory class trains marketing. Just take the Expedition class and save some money. The view is just as beautiful. It ok to miss out on the extra 2 inch window size and complimentary snack and fashion show!. I took Expedition and Vistadome (to and from) just to compare and that is my opinion. 4> Choose seats early if possible and take the left side seats for better views of the valley. You are not missing out on anything by taking the right side seats. Left is just slightly better.

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: Machu Picchu is, without a doubt, one of the most incredible places I’ve ever visited—possibly the most awe-inspiring place on the planet. Perched high in the Andes Mountains, it feels completely surreal, like you’ve stepped into a dream or a world untouched by time. From the moment you arrive and the clouds part to reveal the ruins, you’re hit with a sense of wonder and reverence that’s hard to put into words. The location is jaw-droppingly beautiful—surrounded by steep, lush peaks, with the Urubamba River winding far below—and the energy of the place is unlike anything else. The architecture and engineering are beyond impressive. The Incas built with such precision and purpose that it’s hard to believe this was all done without modern tools. The stones fit together so tightly that not even a blade of grass can slide between them. Every building, terrace, and path has meaning—whether it’s astronomical, agricultural, or spiritual. The Temple of the Sun, the Intihuatana Stone, the perfectly aligned solstice windows—it all shows how advanced and in tune with nature this civilization really was. What truly elevates Machu Picchu from a historical site to a life-changing destination is the feeling it gives you. There’s a quiet, sacred atmosphere here. Even with other tourists around, there are moments of total silence where it feels like the mountains are speaking. I found myself stopping often—not just to admire the view, but to take it all in on a deeper level. Sitting near the edge, watching mist drift across the stones, listening to the wind, you realize this is more than a ruin—it’s a reminder of how small we are and how powerful human creativity and connection to the Earth can be. If you’re able, I highly recommend hiking up Huayna Picchu or Machu Picchu Mountain. The trails are challenging, but the views from the top are absolutely unforgettable. You’ll see the ruins from above, laid out like a cosmic map between the clouds and peaks. It’s breathtaking. Whether you get there by train or take the multi-day trek along the Inca Trail, just getting to Machu Picchu is an adventure—and totally worth it. The entire experience is unforgettable from start to finish. This is not just a place to see—it’s a place to feel. It stays with you, long after you’ve left. If you’re thinking of going, stop thinking and just go. Machu Picchu isn’t just a destination. It’s a soul-stirring, humbling, and deeply moving experience that will leave you changed.

text_clean: Machu Picchu is, without a doubt, one of the most incredible places I’ve ever visited—possibly the most awe-inspiring place on the planet. Perched high in the Andes Mountains, it feels completely surreal, like you’ve stepped into a dream or a world untouched by time. From the moment you arrive and the clouds part to reveal the ruins, you’re hit with a sense of wonder and reverence that’s hard to put into words. The location is jaw-droppingly beautiful—surrounded by steep, lush peaks, with the Urubamba River winding far below—and the energy of the place is unlike anything else. The architecture and engineering are beyond impressive. The Incas built with such precision and purpose that it’s hard to believe this was all done without modern tools. The stones fit together so tightly that not even a blade of grass can slide between them. Every building, terrace, and path has meaning—whether it’s astronomical, agricultural, or spiritual. The Temple of the Sun, the Intihuatana Stone, the perfectly aligned solstice windows—it all shows how advanced and in tune with nature this civilization really was. What truly elevates Machu Picchu from a historical site to a life-changing destination is the feeling it gives you. There’s a quiet, sacred atmosphere here. Even with other tourists around, there are moments of total silence where it feels like the mountains are speaking. I found myself stopping often—not just to admire the view, but to take it all in on a deeper level. Sitting near the edge, watching mist drift across the stones, listening to the wind, you realize this is more than a ruin—it’s a reminder of how small we are and how powerful human creativity and connection to the Earth can be. If you’re able, I highly recommend hiking up Huayna Picchu or Machu Picchu Mountain. The trails are challenging, but the views from the top are absolutely unforgettable. You’ll see the ruins from above, laid out like a cosmic map between the clouds and peaks. It’s breathtaking. Whether you get there by train or take the multi-day trek along the Inca Trail, just getting to Machu Picchu is an adventure—and totally worth it. destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: The mysterious Nazca lines are an unusual sight that will leave visitors with a sense of awe. These huge images on the desert floor were relatively undiscovered until planes flying over the area in the 1920s saw the lines from the air and realized they formed distinct patterns and images. Until that time there was some recognition of the hillside drawings near Nazca and Paracas, which can be seen from ground level. However, the huge drawings on the flat desert floor are so large that it requires an aerial view to be appreciated. From the air, it is possible to see 70 different plant and animal drawings as well as hundreds of lines and other geometrical shapes. Some of these lines stretch as long as 10 kilometers, and they are spread over hundreds of square kilometers. Most notable among the figures are a lizard measuring 180 meters long, a condor with a 130-meter wingspan, and several others that include a monkey, hummingbird, killer whale, and spider. Although it is not known exactly who created the lines or how and why, theories hold that the lines were the product of the Paracas and Nazca cultures sometime between 900 BC and AD 600. Why they were created is the subject of much debate. Some of the theories put forward suggest the lines were a type of astronomical calendar for agriculture, an alien landing pad, a running track, walkways joining ceremonial sites, or part of a water cult. The lines were created by removing the dark surface layer of stones and piling them at the sides of the lines, creating a contrast between the dark stones and the exposed lighter soil below. Flights can be booked in advance or on a walk-in, first-come first-serve basis. Approximately four kilometers outside of Nazca are the Cantalloc Aqueducts. Built around AD 300 to 600, the aqueducts were designed to provide a year-round water source for the area. They conduct water from the mountain springs down to Nazca by means of underground canals. Some of the Cantalloc Aqueducts are still used by farmers in the area. Also of interest in the area is the Cemetery of Chauchilla, which contains Nazca remains and mummies

text_clean: These mountaintop ruins are breathtaking, and the stone construction of the houses, temples, and walls are superb. The only problem is that half the site, including the iconic front walls and gateway, are currently under construction and inaccessible. There is a cable car recently built from the town of Nuevo Tingo to Kuelap, but it was not working the day I went, so we had to take a private vehicle and then hike up the very top of the mountain to get there. But once things are up and running again, getting to Kuelap should be much easier, and the site is well worth a visit.

No deberian estar ahi, otra cosa es que hay demasiados juegos y le quitan protagonismo a la esencia del parque, ya que antes los niños iban a divertirse con el agua, ahora lo único que les llama la atencion es la cantidad de juegos que han puesto por todo lugar.

text_clean: Don’t bother. The site is closed and you can’t even walk along the base of the wall. You pretty much can get to a fence near where the entrance would have been. It sounds like it will be literally years at best before the site reopens. None of the guides or tour operators or people running the site want to admit that their ‘premiere site’ for northern Peru is closed to tourism for possibly a decade. I would vaguely guess they will need to build a roof over the entire structure at some point to keep stormwater from destabilizing the wall any further. Unless the want to tear sections of the wall down, put a drainage system behind the wall, and rebuild it with a modern support / drainage system behind the walls. Anyways this section of the country is fantastic, and you should totally visit it. But don’t pretend you are going to be able to walk around the top of Kuelap anytime this decade.

Pero pilla muy a desmano para solo media hora de sobrevuelo y ninguna explicación. Nos explicaron mil veces más cosas en Paracas sobre la única figura del candelabro que se encuentra allí, que en todas estas figuras en Nazca.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: "If you're looking for a unique and unforgettable experience in Peru, I highly recommend visiting the Nasca Plateau. The expansive desert landscape is truly awe-inspiring, and the ancient lines etched into the ground will leave you in wonder. It's a testament to the ingenuity and skill of the Nasca people who created them so long ago. The best way to fully appreciate the magnitude and intricacy of the lines is from above, with a scenic flight. The pilots are knowledgeable and provide fascinating insights into the history, culture, and mystery surrounding the lines. Overall, the Nasca Plateau is a must-visit destination for anyone interested in ancient civilizations and mysteries of the world."

text_clean: Infos/Tips: - Entrance is 45 SOL, but it’s worth it - There are audio guides through the iOS and Android applications "Monasterio de Santa Catalina" (Note that it supports Spanish, English and French, and will take the phone’s configured language) - It takes at least 2 hours to do the full tour of it (we took 2 hours and 15 minutes) - I would recommend to do it when it’s sunny/hot in the day, as it’s quite "cooler" in the museum - They accept VISA Founded in 1579 by Maria de Guzman, the Monastery of Santa Catalina is a sprawling complex that covers over 20,000 square meters. It served as a cloister for nuns of the Dominican Second Order and is renowned for its vibrant architecture and well-preserved colonial-era buildings. The monastery features a unique blend of Spanish colonial and native architectural styles, prominently using sillar, the white volcanic stone typical of Arequipa. The buildings are characterized by brightly colored walls in shades of blue, orange, and red, which create a striking visual impact and are complemented by beautiful arches, courtyards, and fountains. The monastery is organized into different sectors, including living quarters, chapels, plazas, streets, and gardens. Each sector served a specific purpose, reflecting the monastic life of prayer, work, and community.

text_clean: Of course a beautiful experience whilst there. If you want to read how fantastic the ruins are, this review is not for you. The entire process of booking any tickets (Machu Picchu) is very problematic. We tried o do advanced booking for the visit in February for a July visit from UK. The site would not allow us to use any UK credit cards. Ultimately, we used a roaming VPN (USA based), and then booked it. I think that is frankly poor tourism management by the government. I would not recommend planning the trip without advanced booking (there were long serpent like queues from 6 am for tickets the next day!!). Of course the day was well spent, We used the services of a local guide (try to use pesos in cash, though they use dollars. It's cheaper being 50 sols Vs 15 or 20 dollars per head).

text_clean: The guards act irresponsibly and put the guests' health at risk by taking away their water. Attached is a photo of the entrance where this happend. We did Circuit 3 and got through the entrance of circuit 3/4 without any problems. When we entered again (through the entrance to the Mountain, which is also for Circuit 1+2) our backpack was suddenly too big. We had (for 2 people) 3l of water in a drinking system built into the backpack, 2 breads in a foldable can, sunscreen and 2 rain jackets. So nothing big, no plastic waste and our backpack fit in the small model box. We discussed in Spanish whether they really wanted to take away our water for the strenuous hike up the mountain in the midday heat, but they remained adamant. This irresponsible behavior inevitably leads to dehydration, which is dangerous since there is no way to buy more water. Otherwise the purchased ticket will be useless. I am horrified by the conditions there and how carelessly they provoke medical emergencies.

text_clean: With all of the beauty of Machu Pichu you need to understand that taking the first 1-3 busses at Dawn is critical to your day. My first day I made the mistake of hiking at 6:30 am. Big mistake! When I arrived I thought I was at Disneyland on a holiday weekend. Many people that can’t walk, perhaps first time in a pair of hiking boots.. the crowds were incredible. As day 1 wore on, it looked like an Instagram/ Facebook selfy-fest!!! Day two, I rose at 4 am and caught the second bus. Two different experiences! Do yourself a favor and get there early!! Sorry to sound bitter, but the number of tickets should be cut by 50% Take a peek at photo #10, my buddy took to understand what 5:30 am lineup looks like 😳

text_clean: I’m giving a 1-star review for the absolutely terrible new ticketing system. Forcing visitors to see only one tiny section of Machu Picchu is a horrendous idea, and if other world wonders followed this model, world tourism would collapse. My experience was a complete disaster. I originally had a ticket for Circuit No. 2 and wanted to go to the top, but my official tour guide insisted the top was part of Circuit No. 1. So I purchased an additional ticket for Circuit No. 1 just to make sure I could see everything. Then, when I tried to go up, the staff told me that what I wanted to see was not Ticket No. 1 but it was actually Circuit No. 2, and because my original Circuit No. 2 ticket had been valid in the morning, it was now “expired”. So despite buying different tickets, they still refused to let me see the top of circuit 2 from Machu Picchu. Their only response was to tell me to complain to my tour guide, which is absolutely useless and does nothing to fix the fact that I ended up out of one important part I came to see. What makes this even worse is their complete lack of empathy. They don’t understand, or probably don’t care, that people wait months or even years to visit Machu Picchu, and for many it’s a once-in-a-lifetime experience. If someone has a VALID ticket, even if it expired, DURING LOW SEASONS, a few hours earlier due to confusing instructions, the staff should show some humanity and let them in. There is absolutely no reason to be so strict. It was even late afternoon and there wasn't any crowd at all. Instead, the rangers and gate agents were rude, unhelpful, inflexible, and completely indifferent. They just recite the same memorized line "your ticket expired" and refuse to help in any real way. Honestly, AI robots would do a far better job than the staff currently working there. I had heard many complaints about Machu Picchu, but now I've experienced this myself. Hope this review helps someone

text_clean: The site itself is exactly what you see in the photos. The views around are nice. It's very crowded though, and buying tickets is a bureaucratic nightmare: You have to queue for a number that allows you to buy a ticket at a certain time. Then have to come back at that time, listen for your number (which is called out by some guy in Spanish to a crowd of hundreds), then queue again. Then you have to pick one of 6 routes and 3 additions, most of which will no longer be available in the upcoming days, depending on your luck. The routes that are available usually don't allow entrance to the actual site since those are booked way in advance via a terrible online system. If you want to go at the start of the year, you cannot book tickets in advance, because you can only by tickets for the current year. Also, this online system is down or unresponsive for most of January. Even though we booked through an agency 6 months in advance, they were not able to secure our tickets so we had to queue. In the end, our group were not able to actually visit the site, only take a photo from top.... There are so many nice things to see in Peru and South America... Including much larger and more impressive Inca settlements, which are much cheaper or even free to visit... It's really a shame the Peruvian makes it so difficult to visit this place. Unless you really really want to visit Machu Picchu, I would recommend spending your time elsewhere.

Then have to come back at that time, listen for your number (which is called out by some guy in Spanish to a crowd of hundreds), then queue again. Then you have to pick one of 6 routes and 3 additions, most of which will no longer be available in the upcoming days, depending on your luck. The routes that are available usually don't allow entrance to the actual site since those are booked way in advance via a terrible online system. If you want to go at the start of the year, you cannot book tickets in advance, because you can only by tickets for the current year. Also, this online system is down or unresponsive for most of January. Even though we booked through an agency 6 months in advance, they were not able to secure our tickets so we had to queue. In the end, our group were not able to actually visit the site, only take a photo from top.... There are so many nice things to see in Peru and South America... Including much larger and more impressive Inca settlements, which are much cheaper or even free to visit... It's really a shame the Peruvian makes it so difficult to visit this place. Unless you really really want to visit Machu Picchu, I would recommend spending your time elsewhere.
label: negativo

Las carreteras dan lástima, los carros y bugys se meten en la playa y por las dunas ( donde viven y anidan muchas especies) y para colmo el centro de interpretación está cerrado por "mantenimiento" . El baño tampoco está incluido en el precio de la entrada. Una vergüenza de reserva, solo es un saca dinero para los turistas. Los paisajes y las playas son muy bonitas y hay muchas aves en la zona

Las carreteras dan lástima, los carros y bugys se meten en la playa y por las dunas ( donde viven y anidan muchas especies) y para colmo el centro de interpretación está cerrado por "mantenimiento" . El baño tampoco está incluido en el precio de la entrada. Una vergüenza de reserva, solo es un saca dinero para los turistas. Los paisajes y las playas son muy bonitas y hay muchas aves en la zona
label: negativo

A veces está muy apurado y solo hace una descripción del sitio sin profundizar en la historia.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: This is a cool historical site surrounded by sea of police corruption. We were there in December and five (5) police shakedowns later, my son and I made it from the Nazca desert back to Lima. Our strategies included dividing up the $100 dollars the Hilton gave us on credit (after the police stole all of our money a day earlier). We divided the $ and hid it in various locations in the car. Each time I would reload my wallet with only $20 dollars. Each time the Peru Police pulled us over and demanded money for not having a fire extinguisher in the car, for having the lights on, for NOT having the lights on...they just pulled out their little book of violations and pointed to a violation and demanded payment on the spot. Each time my wallet only had $20 and we said that was all we had and we would be happy to pay by credit card (which of course they could not take...). We kept our original passports hidden and just used the copies of our documents that the Hilton made for us. We would say that "the police already kept our original documents and we don't have them anymore and are going to the American Consulate in Lima to ask for them to be replaced." One group of police actually let us go with no payment at all...so that was positive.... I've been to 84 countries. This is the most corrupt place that I have ever seen. Sometimes the traffic fines come with death threats. Seeing the lines by aircraft was interesting but not worth the price of all the police terrorism to get down there from Lima.

text_clean: Recientemente visité la Plaza Mayor de Lima y me pareció muy pequeña en comparación con otras plazas principales de ciudades similares. Aunque es un lugar histórico y emblemático, me esperaba algo más grande y majestuoso. Sin embargo, es un lugar lleno de vida y energía, con hermosos edificios y una gran cantidad de restaurantes y tiendas. Creo que es un lugar que vale la pena visitar, pero no esperen una plaza grandiosa.

text_clean: Creo que no hay relación en el costo y el tiempo que te lleva llegar al Machu Picchu, con lo que es. Obvio que es hermoso! Y hay que conocerlo, pero hay muchísima gente, compré el circuito 3 por el waina Picchu, una guia nos cobro 50 soles , que si es necesaria la explicación, pero este circuito son 200 mts? ...luego subis al waina que es espectacular, cansador y no apto para los que tienen miedo a las alturas, ahí es libre el tiempo, pero después cuando bajas , seguís por tu circuito hacia la salida, pero sin explicación de guía y toda la gente saliendo hace que te vayas rápido. El pueblo de aguas calientes me pareció muy lindo!!! Muchos bares, eso sí, todo en inglés, está bien que haya turistas, pero una carta en el resto en castellano!!! Y lo más importante....fui en minivan desde Cusco a hidroeléctrica, el camino es un desastre, nose como está habilitada esa ruta!!! Luego camine 10 km, lindo...pero obviamente volví en tren ( 70 dólares) no iba a arriesgar mi vida , menos que siempre llueve.... Conclusión es caro? Si...hay que conocerlo? Si...peroooo hay otros lugares que valen la pena, esto es puro comercio.

text_clean: A very good place to visit in the north of Perú. It is one of the most fascinating archaeology sites in all of the country, and definitely up there in the world as well. Unbelievably hidden from the outside world for many hundreds of years until a local pastor came across it under the brush and now have it displayed for the world to enjoy. They say the city is not in a fortress, so perhaps it is best described as a walled city, but one could imagine what that might look like. It is deceivingly small from the outside as you have to walk around it to get in, then once you’re inside, you realise how large it actually is. Admission is s/25, or s/15 for students like myself. The site can be accessed by driving or for those that do not mind heights so much, the cable car (telecabina). Would highly recommend to anyone interesting in the Chachapoyas people and history.

text_clean: I came to Arequipa to enjoy the city and to visit the Monastery was something I was excited about doing. I arrived yesterday and I was extremely disappointed to find that there was no way for someone like me to enter and enjoy this beautiful piece of architecture and history.. I have muscular dystrophy and to use stairs is basically impossible for me. I understand that it is a historic city and things sometimes can’t be changed for preservation purposes - but to have no ramp or mini platform elevator or alternate entrance or even a handrail for the stairs was shocking and disappointing to say the least. I only write this review to let others know who may be physically altered (senior citizens or pregnant women) or handicapped like me that this will be what you find when you arrive there. It makes no sense for a place that is so historical and beautiful and important, a tourist treasure of the city, to not be accesible to everyone. I was still able to enjoy some of the beautiful architecture from the outside, but it was very sad not to be able to experience this.

text_clean: Tuve la oportunidad de visitar el lugar en 2018 y, en ese momento, fue una experiencia única. Pude hacer el recorrido saliendo de Cusco y regresando el mismo día. Lamentablemente, hoy el costo de ingreso se ha incrementado de manera considerable y, con el nuevo sistema de circuitos, ya no es posible recorrer todo el sitio. Prácticamente es obligatorio quedarse al menos una noche en Machu Picchu Pueblo para subir al día siguiente, lo que hace muy difícil realizar todo el recorrido en una sola jornada. El lugar sigue siendo increíble, pero la experiencia ya no se siente igual.

text_clean: My rating and comments are not related to the Machu Picchu itself (which is definitely one of the wonder of the world) but by the way visits are organized. It has unfortunately turned into a tourist zoo -it’s seem to be more daily tourists than the theoretical 2500 tickets allowance -visit must follow a one way buckle path, forget about following your inspiration to walk around -you must cross and queue within an horrible and crowded tourist village located around the train station -be also ready to queue within train station, to get on board the bus to go down, to enter the sight... -and obviously to contribute generously to Peruvian government budget

Pues bien, no se parece en nada. Para empezar es como un pueblito, con sus calles y sus casas, por cierto, las calles tienen nombre de ciudades españolas, Córdoba, Sevilla, Granada,..... En cada casa vivían hasta tres monjas que eran de la misma familia y esas casas eran construidas esa familia, tenían servicio..... .. En fin es muy largo de contar. En Arequipa es visita obligada.

Dentro hay una cafetería con precios normales, el monasterio es enorme y puede tomar horas recorrerlo. Asegúrate de llevar zapatos cómodos, algo de tomar y snacks.

text_clean: As a tourist you really do not need to join a tour to visit the gardens. I took an Uber from Miraflores which was c.20s, paid the 5s entrance fee and additional 5s entrance for the k pop area and finally a c.20s Uber back to Miraflores. So I spent 50s in total vs tours which are 3-4x the price. As per other reviews, some fountains are shut for maintenance and all the fountains have metal barriers around; so it’s not the best atmosphere. The light show at 7.15 was good!

text_clean: Bring your nighttime equipped cameras and recording equipment, this place is truly amazing and whimsical. The dancing water fountains showcased through lights and music are a joy and spectacle to behold and are sure to bring a smile to your face. The fountains are spread out in a meandering walkway ( a circuit) that start small and culminate at a majestic grand fountain showcasing a dancing water show of synchronized pageantry. The entrance fee is extremely affordable and well worth to witness this colorful and exuberant public space. All large metropolitan cities should offer a grand park like this to it's citizens to fostering genuine family time that is both genuine, fun and inspiring. A jewel of Lima and the beautiful country of Peru.

text_clean: Excelente lugar para visitar en tu paso por Chachapoyas. Hay sol, pero es agradable, suele llover, recomendable ir con ropa impermeable. Para llegar vas en las telecabinas, una experiencia magnífica, ves lo majestuoso que son los cerros. Para llegar a la fortaleza el camina está señalizado, es en subida unos 300 metros z luego ya es plano. Llegas a la fortaleza y te deja maravillado, tienes una vista impresionante. Te conectas con la naturaleza. Les recomiendo la agencia Kuelap Tours. Ahh, el trato es muy agradable. Me olvidaba de comentarles que antes de subir a la fortaleza hay tiendas para comprar recuerdos, tienen que hacerlo.

text_clean: It was one of the highlights of our 2 weeks tour of Peru. My recommendation is to get there early, as the mid morning sun cast the best light on the white washed sillar walls and the red and indigo interior. Do pay for a guide to explain the rich history behind the convent. The guided tour is done by the official onsite guide and in various languages and it only cost Sol 5 per person and you pay directly to the guide at the end of the guided tour. The guide we had was a female guide by the name of Alexandria and she is very knowledgeable and speaks perfect English. It is so fascinating to know that the convent was somewhat like the “Elite” convent only the rich and famous can afford to send their daughters. Many of the nuns had several (some up to 4-5) maids with them the entire time they were in the monastery! The tour will take about an hour to conclude, after which, spend another hour at least to wonder around the labyrinth-like convent and explore many of the living quarters of the nun. If you have time, maybe a mid-morning coffee in the picturesque cafe. Photo taking is permitted. All in all, plan to spend at least 2-2.5 hours here. It is very much well worth the time!

text_clean: The Colca Canyon is a canyon of the Colca River in southern Peru, located about 160 kilometres northwest of Arequipa. With a depth of about 1000 - 2000 m, it is one of the deepest canyons in the world. Its length is about 70 kilometres. The Colca Valley is a colorful Andean valley with pre-Inca rooted inhabitants, and towns founded in Spanish colonial times, still inhabited by people of the Collagua and the Cabana cultures. The local people maintain their ancestral traditions and continue to cultivate the pre-Inca stepped terraces, called andenes. It is Peru's third most-visited tourist destination with about 120,000 visitors annually.

text_clean: 1) YOU CANNOT WALK BACK, OR REPEAT ANY PART OF YOUR CIRCUIT. There are staff watching and they will blow their whistle and shout at you if you try. We arrived in a downpour so we walked to some shelter and waited it out. When we tried to walk back on the circuit we had paid for, we were not allowed. It doesn't matter that it's not busy, you want to take photos or any other reason, they will not let you walk back, even 20m. Unfortunately this was not clear when we purchased the ticket so we missed a significant portion of our route :( Therefore I'd advise you to walk slowly and enjoy the views, because once you arrive at the exit, that will be the end of your time at macchu pichu. I feel the staff lack compassion since it was 7am and quiet at macchu pichu, and I didn't want it to rain. If they had a cafe inside at the beginning I'd have waited there, but you have to enter at your time slot so I was left in a downpour in my poncho. I'd paid for flights, accomodation, and so many buses and trains to be there. The staff didn't show any compassion for my situation and were curt. <- therefore one star, but then I added one star for the staff that let me go take pictures of the llamas :) 2) TOILETS WILL COST YOU EXTRA ON TOP OF THE ENTRANCE FEE, AND BAGGAGE STORAGE. Which I also find ridiculous considering the entrance ticket is so expensive. Toilet S/2 and baggage is S/5. 3) YOU CANNOT ENTER BEFORE THE ENTRANCE TIME ON YOUR TICKET Something to do with the system they use. 4) DO NOT FLY A DRONE The staff have a walkie talkie system and respond very quickly. I don't know what the consequences are, but the people we saw were taken to the office. 5) DO NOT BOOK EARLY MORNING TIME SLOT IF YOU ARE VISITING IN WET/ SHOULDER SEASONS The clouds tend to hang low in the sky in the morning so you might not have the best view/ you might have to wait for clouds to clear. 6) YOU CAN BUY THE BUS TICKET FOR THE BUS UPWARDS ONLINE, BEFOREHAND (12 USD per ride) Just look for the one bus company that runs the route. I can't remember off the top of my head. You can also purchase them at the bottom and at the top, the process is quick and easy. Hope this helps :) I did route 1&2 and was happy with all the areas it covered.

text_clean: Educate yourselves well on what you want to do on the mountain and where you want to spend your time. (Machu Picchu (4 hours), Huayna Picchu (3 hours) + caves (1hour?), Inca bridge (1 hour), Citadel (1 hour), Sun Gate (2 hours), etc). Our guide wasted almost two hours of our entry time which really limited what our options were for our personal goals in our tired condition. My partner and I did a 4 day/3 night Jungle adventure which involved quite a lot of hiking. By the time we got to Machu Picchu our feet and legs were pretty destroyed. (Air Canada lost her hiking bag so she didn't have hiking boots that were broken in so she was in worse shape than I was). We wanted to climb one or both mountains of time permitted on the site but settled for the Sun Gate and the Citadel. Know your limits. Know what your goals are before you get to Peru.

Afterwards we went round again and there was enough information available on the walls and the leaflet you get given with entry (unlike in several other attractions in Peru) to enjoy a slower self guided tour. That being said, we heard a couple of more diligent guides while on our second lap so perhaps we were just unlucky.

Le daría cinco estrellas por el valor del sitio, la calidad de la guía y el cuidado del jardín de plantas tradicionales; sin embargo, los cuys y las llamas que tienen están en muy mal estado. En particular, los cuys están hacinados en grandes cantidades en espacios pequeños. Están sobre sus propias heces. En el poco tiempo que estuve ahí vi como uno atacaba a otros como resultado de estar tan apretados. No veo la necesidad de tener a estos animalitos en ese espacio tan reducido.

text_clean: Ayer fui al circuito mágico del agua. Una pésima idea, fui con un adulto mayor y niños, no hay asientos disponibles y en una calor extrema, no hay donde taparse o asombrarse del del sol cuando quisimos sentarnos en el en la zonas verdes nos dijeron que estaba prohibido y tuvimos que estar parados cerca de las tres horas que estuvimos con el adulto mayor y no pudimos sentarnos a ningún lugar ya que todo estaba repleto; pero tengo que hacer una observación. Hay muchos juegos para niños que están posicionados en muchas áreas verdes,mientras a la gente que paga una entrada, no se nos permitía ni siquiera sentarnos dentro de estas zonas ya que no hay asientos suficiente, me parece muy pésima idea es un sitio donde uno quiere ir a a observar las aguas, las piletas, etc. pero lo han convertido en un sitio donde se está monetizando y muchas personas que venimos de fuera quedamos decepcionados , Prefiero ir a otros lugares donde te traten mejor. No les sugiero que vayan, tengan otras opciones para poder salir un fin de semana a divertirse acá en Lima.

text_clean: Mis recomendaciones en general: 1. Solo quédense 1 noche a lo mucho en aguas calientes , no hay nada que hacer y todo es carísimo, si pueden hacer el pasadía mejor. 2. Pueden ahorrar se el dinero del bus si caminan a machupichu, es muy lindo y con tiempo suben en unas 1 hora y media y la verdad es lo mejor de ir a machupichu. La dificultad no es alta pese a unos escalones. 3. Compren circuito 2. 4. Los guías son súper groseros sino les compras su tour, somos colombianos y cuando le dijimos a una guía que no queríamos el servicio nos dijo pobres. 5. Es linda la vista pero la logística es muy mala, la gente de los tours se atiborra 6. Lleven gorra, gafas, filtro solar. 7. Compren los tiquetes del ciurcuito un día antes antes de la entrada a machupichu, comprarlo meses antes por la página no es necesario, siempre hay tiquetes (si vas a hacer esto quédate un día antes porque solo así los venden)

text_clean: One of a kind museum. Pay for the guided tour. It's less than $10usd and covers about 1.5hrs of history. They don't let you take cell phones into the museum. First Sunday of all months the 10 soles entrance is free.

text_clean: Nos dió mucha rabia la entrada. Le dice a uno la guía ( Milagros) que uno tiene que reservar con un día de anticipación. text_clean: Most of these mysterious geoglyphs were created by the Nazca people, who lived in the area from 200 to 700 CE. But the researchers believe that some of the newfound ones were created even earlier - by the Paracas and Topará people, who lived there around 500 BCE to 200 CE. The lines have been a source of fascination and wonder for decades. Created by pushing aside the top layer of red desert pebbles to reveal the pale layer underneath, these geoglyphs span vast tracts of the Nazca Desert, between the towns of Nazca and Palpa. Some of them form geometric shapes, some are simple lines, and some are combined into elaborate depictions of animals and objects. The most wondrous thing about these ancient symbols is that you often can't see what they depict from ground level. You have to get up in the air to discern the patterns, which is why their full glory wasn't understood until after the invention of airplanes.

text_clean: Stunning place - but for the life of us we could not work out what the 70 soles per person fee was going to. The whole thing stank of corruption and lining local officials pockets - we were told it was for tourism infrastructure, but this was few and far between. And as a tourist I'm already paying for my hotel, transport and food that is going to benefit the local economy, I'm not completely sure what extra this was going to - just a payment to look at nature? Yes, the roads were good, but I'm pretty sure that's more because of the plethora of mining companies in the area. Seemed just like seeing foreign tourists as open wallets and was definitely not a great side to see of Peru.

text_clean: This the worst ticketing system I’ve ever experienced in my life. There are barely any online tickets. So most mere peasants will have to stand for more than 4 hours in line to get a pre-holding ticket, to wake up the next day early to stand in line again to get now a holding ticket. They are screaming the number of your ticket in Spanish. So I hope for you, that you know your Spanish numbers well. Please keep in mind you still have not even purchased your actual ticket yet. With your holding ticket you can now go back in line to purchase your actual ticket for the day after. It’s incredulous how disgusting and disorganized this system is. Basically wasting at least two days of vacation days to just get a ticket for the 2 hours to 1 day of Machu Pichu that you will be spending. I would urge everyone with limited time to consider whether visiting Machu Pichu is worth it. As there are other beautiful (pre)Incan ruins to see in Cusco, such as Pisac, Olantaymbo etc.

text_clean: Lo ideal es quedarse una o dos noches en alguno de los pueblos cercanos, como Yanque o Chivay, y disfrutar de las profundidades de este paisaje con un coche de alquiler. Hay que ir parando continuamente para hacer fotos o para descubrir a lo lejos algún pueblecito aislado al otro lado del río o algún huerto salpicado de los colores vivos de la ropa de los lugareños. El mirador del cóndor es un hervidero de turistas, no sé cómo estas aves siguen sobrevolando la zona con tanta tranquilidad y no buscan otros pagos.

text_clean: The grand dame of Lima’s history - Plaza Mayor de Lima, where colonial guilt meets postcard charm. Founded in 1535 by Francisco Pizarro (whose statue was exiled, then sneakily reinstated), this place has seen it all.. declarations of independence, public executions, and more balcony drama than a telenovela. Surrounded by impressive facades like the Cathedral, Presidential Palace, and City Hall, it’s like standing in the middle of a well-behaved architectural argument. The bronze fountain in the center has outlived more presidents than Peru can count, and once even served pisco to the masses, viva la fuente! Come for the history, stay for the perfectly photogenic symmetry. But don’t expect solitude, the only thing louder than the tour groups are the pigeons claiming turf like feathered conquistadors. Still, whether you’re admiring colonial craftsmanship or plotting escape from the sun, Plaza Mayor delivers drama, dignity and a dash of Spanish déjà vu.

text_clean: CORRUPTION ALERT! Important: this review is NOT about Machu Picchu itself. Machu Picchu is extraordinary and deserves every bit of its reputation. This review is about the management of the ticketing system and the openly tolerated corruption surrounding it. The current circuit reservation system is a mess. We planned our trip months in advance and specifically wanted Circuit 2. We checked availability online repeatedly, including several months before arrival, and it appeared unavailable. We accepted that and adjusted our plans. Then we arrived in Peru and discovered that there were hundreds of tickets available locally for the very circuits that had supposedly been unavailable online. If tickets exist, they should be visible and accessible through the official system. If they are being intentionally reserved for local sales, that should be clearly explained. The lack of transparency makes the entire process feel arbitrary and unfair. What happened inside Machu Picchu was even worse. The site management constantly emphasizes the importance of circuit controls, visitor limits, and strict regulations to protect the monument. Yet while standing near entrances to other circuits, we were approached multiple times by guides who openly suggested they could get us access in exchange for extra cash. We did not ask for this. We were not looking for loopholes. The offers came to us. Not once. Not twice. Multiple times. The most disturbing part was how normal it seemed. There was no hesitation, no attempt to hide it, and guides appeared to be communicating with one another. It gave the impression that everyone knows it is happening and nobody cares enough to stop it. So what exactly is the purpose of all these strict regulations if they can be bypassed with a cash payment? Visitors are told that access restrictions are essential to prevent overcrowding and protect one of the world’s most important archaeological sites. Yet at the same time, people are openly offering ways around those restrictions for money. That is not conservation. That is not heritage protection. That is a system that risks undermining its own credibility. Machu Picchu deserves better than this. The people responsible for managing access should focus less on creating confusing ticketing rules and more on addressing the corruption that visitors can see with their own eyes. If employees, guides, or contractors are openly soliciting unofficial payments at one of the world’s most famous cultural sites, that should be treated as a serious problem and investigated immediately. The monument is incredible. The management experience was deeply disappointing.

text_clean: Como muchos otros comentan, ya se perdió la esencia de lo que era el “Circuito mágico del agua”, ahora parece una feria o parque de diversiones donde la atracción principal son esos juegos llenos de luces y ruido en lugar de las fuentes de agua que solían ser lo que todos íbamos a ver. Me da mucha pena como está todo ahora, porque ya casi ni hay áreas verdes a la vista, todo es kiosko, juegos mecánicos o inflables (y para colmo, a precios elevadísimos). Como cereza del pastel, ya ni te dejan ver el total de fuentes de agua si no pagas antes 5 soles extra para cruzar el túnel. Pésimo como arruinaron el parque de las aguas.

text_clean: For me, it's an Overrated marketing success of an abandoned settlement. I will submit that the majestic mountains and valleys are the main draw and attraction. The hyped settlement architecture? It is just a cluster of abandoned stone/rock/mud low 'buildings' meant for 3-400 families. Sure, some walls are tightly stacked with big chipped/carved boulders/rocks and even a blade can't get thru the gaps... The mountain terrace carved/landscaped for agriculture is not unique to the Inca. The use of gravity to channel water is common amongst other old civilisations too... With a good guide, the story telling of Inca civilisation and the Inca connection to mother earth and nature can be engaging. Know too that getting there to MP is a long journey and time wasting pain, compounded with an eventual convoluted Q system to get expensive tickets. (It can be a 2 day Q for a 3hrs just one section visit of MP). .. And a tourist trap village venue to take a bus up to MP. When there, Also beware the nasty midges/sand flies which can carry the deadly oropouch virus. Only this is free. 🤣😂 Oh, the story of the hacked down obelisk to accommodate the Prez helicopter landing is outlandish.

With a good guide, the story telling of Inca civilisation and the Inca connection to mother earth and nature can be engaging. Know too that getting there to MP is a long journey and time wasting pain, compounded with an eventual convoluted Q system to get expensive tickets. (It can be a 2 day Q for a 3hrs just one section visit of MP). .. And a tourist trap village venue to take a bus up to MP. When there, Also beware the nasty midges/sand flies which can carry the deadly oropouch virus. Only this is free. 🤣😂 Oh, the story of the hacked down obelisk to accommodate the Prez helicopter landing is outlandish.
label: negativo

text_clean: Amazing ruins of the ancient holy city of Incas. The place doesn't need any explanation, it's among 7 world wonders and very well known. However I believe the local authorities did exploited this place too much without caring too much about the experience of the visit. Although the daily capacity of visitors is capped this place looks way to busy and filled with people who are just there to make selfies. My suggestion is to reach it via Inca trail, even just the last piece of the trekking, so that you can see it from the top of Porta del Sol. Lastly the entry costs have crazly skyrocket in last 10 years with little if no sense.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Esta localizado en Lambayeque Perú. Su diseño arquitectónico tiene similitud con las Piramides preincaicas de la cultura Mochica. El objetivo del museo es exhibir la tumba del señor de Sipan que fue descubierta en 1987 por los arqueólogos peruanos. Eate museo es de tres pisos, desde el segundo piso se observa la tumba exacta de una cámara funebre del antiguo dignatario. En el centro del museo se puede apreciar la sala Real Mochica donde se encuentra el Señor de Sipan con su rropa de guerrero y pectoral de oro además de otras joyas.

Subir por la vía peatonal es libre y se tarda desde 40 minutos a 1 hora dependiendo del estado físico. Es muy ordenado el ingreso y si no tienes guía (que es obligatorio) en la entrada hay muchos ofreciendo el servicio desde $10. text_clean: Machu Picchu Is undoubtedly one of the most incredible places we've ever been too, helped by good weather and a quieter day with fewer people. However, there's a few things though that could be done to improve the experience, particularly given the hefty price tag attached to the visit. - There is zero information around the site about it's history or design. When visiting one of the wonders of the world it seems insane that zero information is provided to let people learn more about the sites rich past. I presume this is to encourage the use of guides, but regardless a small number of boards, even just indicating what certain spaces are or what they were used for, should be a minimum. - Toilets should be free. Given the amount it costs, there is no excuse for charging for the toilets at the top of the hill. If required, get people to show their tickets before being able to use the bathroom, but to outright charge is just a desperate attempt to squeeze you for a couple more dollars

text_clean: Hoy descubrí la historia del señor de sipán El museo un amplio complejo muy agradable con una zona trasera con casetas de artesanos La entrada se paga al entrar al recinto, para meter el coche son dos soles y la entrada de adultos son 10 soles No se puede acceder ni con móviles, cámaras ni bolsos al museo. Las taquillas las tienen en el exterior( cosa poco usual y de primeras un poco desconcertante, en fin) Puedes pagar por un guia 30soles. El museo se divide en tres plantas, no es muy grande se ve en una hora aproximadamente. Lo peor que vi es la oscuridad que tiene el museo. Las vitrinas con texto no se puede leer bien.

text_clean: Sugiero hacer reserva al número que está en su página. No pude apreciar la vista de la huaca, mi mesa estaba muy lejos y al parecer dan preferencia a grupos para ubicarlos cerca. Yo fui solo con mi pareja. La atención del personal no fue de lo más amable tomando en cuenta el renombre del restaurante, uno espera mayor cordialidad y calidez. La comida estuvo muy buena, ordenamos un lomo saltado y un tacu tacu con sábana de lomo. Y de postre, esfera de café y caramelo. Los tragos también estuvieron en su punto. No les recomiendo ir en parejas si desean algo más romántico, creo que hay opciones mejores; pero, para reunirse en grupo y celebrar algo especial sí lo valdría.

text_clean: Two stars compared to my expectations. It's a beautiful place, but overrun with people, expensive to get to, and v touristy. Think Disney meets Williamsburg. I did enjoy the hike up Machu Picchu mountain and the conversations with other travelers. We took the bus up, and hiked back down. The hike up is a serious climb, so be prepared! If you'd like to avoid the train, you can get a bus to a hydroelectric plant from Cusco and, from there, hike a couple hours to Aguas Calientes. Overall, the guides we heard didn't have knowledge that made it worth the cost. I'd recommend and audio tour online instead. There are more beautiful places and hikes to be found in the world.

text_clean: Para ir al machu picchu tienes que comprar las entradas con 2 o 3 meses de antelacion. He ido con mi familia y no es lo que esperaba, tienes que pagar por todo, si vas a subir a las montañas tienes que ir preparado con mucha agua y ropa comoda. Además para entrar al machu picchu tienes que pagar por circuitos, antes no hacia falta ya que con el circuito 1 se podia ver todo, ahora tienes que pagar por ver cada cosa, por ejemplo: en el circuito 1 solo ves el machu picchu desde fuera y subir la montaña, nada más. Si subes las montañas es bajo tu responsabilidad y si te caes o te haces daño tienes que pagar mucho dinero para un medico (creo que mas de 100 dolares). Tampoco es que al subir la montaña haiga una recompensa tremenda, subi la montaña del circuito 1 y solo habian unas sillas y un letrero de cuanta altura tiene la montaña. Puedes sacar buenas fotos pero nada más, si no traes agua, mejor ni lo intentes, tienes tiempo limite hasta las 12:00 mediodia para subir y se tarda unas 4 horas a paso lento. Si vas a subir tambien recomiendo llevar repelentes para los mosquitos ya que hay muchos mosquitos. Si te gusta escalar este viaje es para ti, pero si eres un poco sedentario no vale la pena pagar tanto solo para subir una montaña y tomarte unas cuantas fotos, por cierto, si llueve olvidate de subir los cerros, es imposible y probablemente te rompas o te hagas daño o peor. No hay seguridad alguna. Sinceramente es bonito para pasar el dia pero tienes que comprar los demas circuitos (1,2,3,4) , porque si no, no puedes ver nada. Recalco otra vez, tienes que pagar por todo, TODO. Las fotos son preciosas pero no todo lo que brilla es oro. Se podria distribuir mejor y que todo no sea tan caro. Igualmente muy bonito Perú y hermoso el machu picchu aunque con lo que cobran se podria mejor muchisimo.

Tampoco es que al subir la montaña haiga una recompensa tremenda, subi la montaña del circuito 1 y solo habian unas sillas y un letrero de cuanta altura tiene la montaña. Puedes sacar buenas fotos pero nada más, si no traes agua, mejor ni lo intentes, tienes tiempo limite hasta las 12:00 mediodia para subir y se tarda unas 4 horas a paso lento. Si vas a subir tambien recomiendo llevar repelentes para los mosquitos ya que hay muchos mosquitos. Si te gusta escalar este viaje es para ti, pero si eres un poco sedentario no vale la pena pagar tanto solo para subir una montaña y tomarte unas cuantas fotos, por cierto, si llueve olvidate de subir los cerros, es imposible y probablemente te rompas o te hagas daño o peor. No hay seguridad alguna. Sinceramente es bonito para pasar el dia pero tienes que comprar los demas circuitos (1,2,3,4) , porque si no, no puedes ver nada. Recalco otra vez, tienes que pagar por todo, TODO. Las fotos son preciosas pero no todo lo que brilla es oro. Se podria distribuir mejor y que todo no sea tan caro. Igualmente muy bonito Perú y hermoso el machu picchu aunque con lo que cobran se podria mejor muchisimo.
label: negativo

text_clean: Unfortunately, our experience visiting Machu Picchu did not meet our expectations. First, one must take a train to get there. The ticket prices are quite high, yet the trains are old and unreliable. Our departure was delayed by an hour because the train broke down en route. The train stations are small and cannot accommodate the number of people waiting, forcing many to sit on the floor or stand until boarding. Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.

text_clean: Es posible visualizar tres de las figuras desde el mirador instalado a la vera de la carretera Panamericana. Sus líneas son de unos 20 cm de ancho y el tamaño de las imágenes es fácilmente visible desde baja altura. El mantenimiento del sitio es escaso, con basura acumulada, baños en estado higiénico terrible, y tejido plástico que perturban la vista de la figura que se encuentra atravesada por la carretera. It is possible to view three of the figures from the viewpoint installed on the side of the Pan-American Highway. Its lines are about 20 cm wide and the size of the images is easily visible from low height. The maintenance of the site is scarce, with accumulated garbage, bathrooms in terrible hygienic state, and plastic fabric that disturb the view of the figure that is crossed by the road.

text_clean: I recently visited Machu Picchu with my partner, and while the site itself is undeniably breathtaking, I am deeply concerned about the lack of proper emergency response systems in place for visitors experiencing medical emergencies. During our visit, my partner, as he got out of the bus, slipped because of the uneven surface and slippery as the road was wet, he injured his ankle. He suffered a concerning medical episode— things unfolded very quickly - he was in a lot of pain, he collapsed and experienced convulsions - due to what we believe was a combination of altitude, and intense pain of the injury. In such a high-traffic and physically demanding site, we expected a prompt and trained medical response. Unfortunately, that was not the case. There was no visible or accessible medical staff nearby, and it took far too long to get any form of assistance. The staff present seemed untrained to handle emergencies such as this and there was no coordinated protocol to help us manage the situation. Thankfully my partner recovered but that was a very stressful moment for us. I am writing this not in anger, but in hopes that the management will: Review and strengthen their emergency protocols. Ensure that trained medical staff are stationed on-site in different posts at all times Install clearly marked emergency points or communication stations Train staff in basic first aid and response procedures Machu Picchu is one of the most visited tourist destinations in the world. With the number of visitors—including elderly people, children, and those not acclimated to the altitude—accidents and health emergencies are not rare, and a more robust system must be put in place to prevent avoidable harm. I truly hope the management takes this seriously. The experience itself is magical, but basic safety and human response must be part of that experience.

text_clean: Worth a visit if in the Arequipa area of Peru. Impressive mountain views an route and we were lucky with the Condor viewings, saw four of them. It is at high altitude so stock up on cocoa leaves first and make sure you know how to use them, they can fend off the altitude sickness if used properly.
